texto: People freely decide how well or how poorly they treat others.  As long as people do not steal from or physically hurt others, there exist no restrictions on social behavior. This means that it is possible that (for instance) someone could be treated very well by everyone, but it also means that (for instance) someone could be treated poorly by everyone. Respond on a 1-7 scale (1 = very unfair, 7 = very fair).
fuente: Meindl, Iyer & Graham (2019), Table S3 (CDJS largo)

texto: The amount and quality of the tangible goods and services people have access to is freely determined by the members of society. People are not guaranteed to have or receive anything, and there are no limits on the amount a person can have (as long as people do not acquire goods of services through theft). Respond on a 1-7 scale (1 = very unfair, 7 = very fair).

texto: The Altaia company has long had the practice of setting aside a portion of the year's profits as a bonus to be divided among its employees using a complex formula that few understand. Some employees have complained that this is not the fairest solution, for a variety of reasons. Please consider the arguments below, and the proposed solutions. Proposal: One employee complained some employees contribute more to the success of the company than others and therefore should receive a larger share of profits. Suppose the company instituted a way of quantifying each employee's contributions, and it then adjusted the bonuses up or down accordingly. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so the total pool of money available for bonuses was unaffected. Rate how desirable it would be to make this change on a 1-7 scale (1 = not desirable, 7 = very desirable).
fuente: Meindl et al. (2019), Table S2 (dilemma; cost-neutral version)

texto: The Altaia company has long had the practice of setting aside a portion of the year's profits as a bonus to be divided among its employees using a complex formula that few understand. Some employees have complained that this is not the fairest solution, for a variety of reasons. Please consider the arguments below, and the proposed solutions. Proposal: One employee complained all employees should get an equal share of the money. Suppose the company divided the money such that each employee received an equal share. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so the total pool of money available for bonuses was unaffected. Rate how desirable it would be to make this change on a 1-7 scale (1 = not desirable, 7 = very desirable).
fuente: Meindl et al. (2019), Table S2 (dilemma; cost-neutral version)

texto: The University of Southern California Business School has long had a practice of requiring students to work on group projects for a significant portion of their grade. Each student receives a grade for their group's work, but the exact criteria used to determine the grade has been unclear. Some students have complained that this is not the fairest solution, for a variety of reasons. Please consider the arguments below, and the proposed solutions. Proposal: One student complained some students contribute more to the success of the project than others and therefore should receive a higher grade. Suppose the school instituted a way of quantifying each student's contribution, and it then adjusted grades up or down accordingly. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so the tuition charged by the school to each student was unaffected. Rate how desirable it would be to make this change on a 1-7 scale (1 = not desirable, 7 = very desirable).
fuente: Meindl et al. (2019), Table S2 (dilemma; cost-neutral version)

texto: The University of Southern California Business School has long had a practice of requiring students to work on group projects for a significant portion of their grade. Proposal: One student complained all students in a group should get an equal grade. Suppose the school gave each student in a group the same grade. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so the tuition charged by the school to each student was unaffected. texto: The John Henry Dean Foundation gives a large grant to a number of charities each year. Each year, the foundation board meets and decides how charities will be funded in the next year, but the exact criteria used to determine how much money each charity receives has been unclear. Some charities have complained that this is not the fairest solution, for a variety of reasons. Please consider the arguments below, and the proposed solutions. Proposal: One charity complained some charities produce better results than others and therefore should receive a larger share of funding. Suppose the foundation instituted a way of quantifying each charities' production, and it then adjusted funding up or down accordingly. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so the total pool of money available for funding was unaffected. Rate how desirable it would be to make this change on a 1-7 scale (1 = not desirable, 7 = very desirable).
fuente: Meindl et al. (2019), Table S2 (dilemma; cost-neutral version)

texto: The Bright Country Day elementary school just bought a new swing set which only accommodates a certain number of kids at recess time. Each recess, a teacher decides which kids get to use the swing, but the exact criteria used to determine who gets to play on the new swing has been unclear. Some parents have complained that this is not the fairest solution, for a variety of reasons. Please consider the arguments below, and the proposed solutions. Proposal: One parent complained some children got better grades in school than others and therefore should receive a larger share of time on the swing. Suppose the school adjusted time on the swing up or down according to each child's grades. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so tuition was unaffected. Rate how desirable it would be to make this change on a 1-7 scale (1 = not desirable, 7 = very desirable).
fuente: Meindl et al. (2019), Table S2 (dilemma; cost-neutral version)
nota: involucra niños (roza cuidado); mérito = notas, algo arbitrario

texto: The World Health Organization has a different president from a different member country each year. Each year, a panel decides which country will pick a representative to be president in the next year, but the exact criteria used to determine which country gets the presidency has been unclear. Some countries have complained that this is not the fairest solution, for a variety of reasons. Please consider the arguments below, and the proposed solutions. Proposal: One country complained some countries contribute more to the success of the organization than others and therefore should receive greater opportunities to be president. Suppose the organization instituted a way of quantifying each country's contributions, and it then adjusted each country's priority level accordingly. Suppose that this adjustment didn't cost anything, so the total pool of money available for programs was unaffected. Rate how desirable it would be to make this change on a 1-7 scale (1 = not desirable, 7 = very desirable).
fuente: Meindl et al. (2019), Table S2 (dilemma; cost-neutral version)
nota: igualdad = igual oportunidad (no resultado); mérito = contribución

texto: The World Health Organization has a different president from a different member country each year. texto: You will decide how a fixed pool of money is distributed among several people for their work.
